products.name: sa20-pack
products.description: Scanner-first SQLAlchemy migration product for high-frequency legacy query and execution API cleanup.
products.summary: Start with the free scan, inspect the report, and only move to the paid pack if the repo is close enough that repetitive cleanup is still expensive.
who_it_is_for: Teams still cleaning up SQLAlchemy 1.4 legacy query patterns.
Repos that still contain Query.get(...), select([..]), string joins, or string loader paths.
Engineering managers who need a narrow, honest migration tool.
proof_points: Public proof shows supported rewrites on real public files.
Fail-closed proof shows engine.execute(...) stays blocked instead of guessed.
The public repo remains scanner-first so a buyer can qualify fit before purchase.
not_for: Repos whose real pain is broad Query-to-select rewriting.
Teams that want a full custom migration service.
Any workflow that expects engine.execute(...) to be auto-fixed everywhere.

products.name: pydantic-v2-porter
products.description: Narrow, deterministic Pydantic v1 to v2 migration product for direct imports, safe validators, supported config keys, and BaseSettings moves.
products.summary: The porter is strongest when the repo still uses direct pydantic imports and a clean validator/settings/config subset.
who_it_is_for: Teams upgrading direct pydantic imports to v2.
Repos dominated by BaseSettings, safe Config, and simple validator cleanup.
Buyers who want deterministic fit assessment before running a paid migration pack.
proof_points: Public proof covers supported validator and settings rewrites on real public repos.
Manual-review findings stay explicit for values-heavy validators.
The paid path stays software-only, with no human coding dependency.
not_for: Repos that rely on alias-heavy imports or import pydantic attribute access.
Validator-heavy code that depends on values, field, config, each_item, or always.
Teams that want a promise of full repo-wide v1 to v2 migration coverage.

products.name: flatconfig-lift
products.description: Static-config migration product for deterministic .eslintrc and package.json eslintConfig cleanup into flat config.
products.summary: This product exists for the static-config subset. It does not pretend to evaluate JS config logic.
who_it_is_for: Teams migrating static .eslintrc JSON or YAML configs.
Repos that still store eslintConfig inside package.json.
Buyers who want a generated FlatCompat bridge instead of a hand-made first pass.
proof_points: Public proof covers static JSON and YAML configs from real public repos.
JS configs and existing flat config files are blocked instead of guessed through.
The bridge output stays close to the source config with machine-generated reporting.
not_for: Repos that already migrated to eslint.config.*.
Teams that want the tool to understand arbitrary plugin ecosystem behavior.

guides.title: Replace session.query(...).get(...) in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: How to replace session.query(...).get(...) in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Fix Query.get migrations and move identity lookups onto Session.get(...).
guides.summary: This is one of the clearest SQLAlchemy 2.0 migrations and a strong codemod target.
guides.answer: If the call is a direct primary-key lookup, the mechanical replacement is Session.get(Model, primary_key).
guides.after_code:
user = session.get(User, user_id)
job = db_session.get(Job, job_id)
symptoms: Legacy Query.get(...) calls still show up in real repos.
Teams want the easy 2.0 fixes first.
Reviewers do not want to hand-edit dozens of lookup calls.
covers: Direct session.query(Model).get(pk) rewrites.
Repo reporting for where the pattern appears.
A scanner-first qualification path.
manual_review: Non-trivial Query chains before .get(...).
Helper wrappers that hide the session object.
Calls that are not direct primary-key lookups.

guides.title: Session.query migration guide for SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: What to do with session.query(...) during a SQLAlchemy 2.0 migration
guides.description: Separate the easy Query cleanup from the broader Query-to-select refactor work.
guides.summary: Query still exists in 2.0, but legacy breakages should be split from the bigger refactor bucket.
guides.answer: Start with deterministic fixes like Query.get(...), string joins, string loader paths, and select([..]) list syntax. Treat broad Query-to-select rewrites as manual review.
guides.after_code:
query = session.query(User)  # still legacy, not the first rewrite target
user = session.get(User, user_id)
rows = session.query(User).join(User.addresses).all()
symptoms: Teams think every Query call must be rewritten at once.
A few safe fixes are buried inside broader churn.
Reviewers need a clean staging plan.
covers: Repo-fit guidance for the safe Query cleanup subset.
Internal links to the exact issue pages.
A scanner-first path before paid tooling.
manual_review: Broad Query-to-select rewrites.
Result-shape changes.
App-specific loader strategy decisions.

guides.title: Fix engine.execute(...) removal in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: How to fix engine.execute(...) removal in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Fix engine.execute removals and OptionEngine errors without pretending the replacement is always trivial.
guides.summary: This is a real migration pain point because there is not one safe automatic replacement.
guides.answer: Move execution onto a Connection or Session and make transaction boundaries explicit. That is why honest tooling buckets engine.execute(...) as manual review.
guides.before_code:
result = engine.execute("SELECT id FROM users")
rows = engine.execute(stmt).fetchall()
guides.after_code:
from sqlalchemy import text

with engine.connect() as conn:
    result = conn.execute(text("SELECT id FROM users"))
    rows = conn.execute(stmt).fetchall()
symptoms: OptionEngine and execute errors after the 2.0 bump.
Old helpers still call engine.execute(...).
Teams need a clear manual-review signal.
covers: Detection and classification of engine.execute(...) usage.
Repo reporting for the manual bucket.
Links to related SQLAlchemy guides.
manual_review: Choosing Connection versus Session.
Raw SQL that needs text(...).
Pandas and helper wrappers around execution.

guides.title: Clean up joinedload_all(...) in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: How to clean up joinedload_all(...) during a SQLAlchemy 2.0 migration
guides.description: Replace joinedload_all(...) with mapped-attribute loader options and bucket the hard cases honestly.
guides.summary: joinedload_all(...) is gone. Simple paths are fixable. Ambiguous paths should stay manual.
guides.answer: Use joinedload(...) with mapped attributes when the relationship path is obvious. Stop when aliasing or dotted paths make the replacement unclear.
guides.after_code:
query = session.query(User).options(
    joinedload(User.orders).joinedload(Order.items)
)
query = query.options(joinedload(User.addresses))
symptoms: Loader options still use string paths.
Teams want to know which paths are easy cleanup.
Reviewers do not want a guessed loader strategy rewrite.
covers: Simple joinedload_all(...) replacements.
Cross-links to string loader cleanup pages.
Fail-closed reporting when the path is not straightforward.
manual_review: Alias-heavy loading.
Dotted paths tied to query shape.
contains_eager and custom join interactions.

guides.title: Fix select([..]) list syntax in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: How to replace select([..]) list syntax in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Drop the legacy select([..]) wrapper and move to direct select(...) calls.
guides.summary: This is exactly the kind of syntax change that good codemods should automate first.
guides.answer: Drop the wrapper list and pass the columns or table directly into select(...).
guides.after_code:
stmt = select(user_table)
stmt = select(User.name, User.email)
symptoms: Legacy list wrapper syntax still appears across multiple files.
The fix is obvious but repetitive.
Teams want proof that the easy cleanup is worth automating.
covers: Deterministic select([..]) to select(..) rewrites.
Repo reporting for the syntax bucket.
Links to the broader SQLAlchemy guide set.
manual_review: Calls already mixed with broader query redesign.
Generated code that obscures the argument list.
Files that also contain unsupported execution-path changes.

guides.title: Replace string join paths in SQLAlchemy 2.0 migrations
guides.h1: How to replace string join paths during a SQLAlchemy 2.0 migration
guides.description: Replace simple join("addresses") and outerjoin("orders") calls with mapped attributes when the root entity is obvious.
guides.summary: Simple string relationship joins are good deterministic cleanup. Multi-hop join chains are not.
guides.answer: When the root entity is obvious, replace the string path with the mapped attribute, like User.addresses or User.orders.
guides.after_code:
query = session.query(User).join(User.addresses)
query = query.outerjoin(User.orders)
symptoms: Legacy Query calls still join relationships by string name.
The right replacement is obvious in the common root-entity case.
Teams do not want to hand-fix dozens of repetitive join calls.
covers: Simple join("relationship") rewrites.
Simple outerjoin("relationship") rewrites.
Reporting for the string-join cleanup bucket.
manual_review: Multi-hop joins like join("orders", "items").
Alias-heavy query builders.
Cases where the root entity is not obvious from the call site.

guides.title: Replace string loader options in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: How to replace string loader options in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Replace joinedload("addresses") and similar string loader options with mapped attributes when the relationship root is obvious.
guides.summary: Simple loader strings are cleanup work. Dotted paths and query-shape-dependent options are not.
guides.answer: For straightforward paths, replace the string with the mapped attribute, like joinedload(User.addresses).
guides.after_code:
query = session.query(User).options(joinedload(User.addresses))
query = query.options(selectinload(User.orders))
symptoms: Loader options still depend on legacy string paths.
The easy cases are repetitive but not conceptually hard.
Teams need a clean stop signal before dotted or aliased paths.
covers: Simple joinedload/lazyload/selectinload/subqueryload string cleanup.
Mapped-attribute replacements when the root entity is obvious.

guides.title: Replace insert(..., values=...) in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: How to replace insert(..., values=...) in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Move legacy insert constructor kwargs onto the statement object with .values(...).
guides.summary: Legacy DML constructor kwargs are repetitive syntax cleanup that good codemods should handle.
guides.answer: Build the statement first, then call .values({...}) on it.
guides.after_code: stmt = insert(user_table).values({"name": name})
symptoms: Insert helpers still rely on constructor kwargs from older SQLAlchemy code.
The fix is obvious but spread across many files.
Teams want the DML syntax cleanup folded into the same migration run.
covers: Direct insert(table, values=...) rewrites.

guides.title: Replace update(..., whereclause=..., values=...) in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: How to replace update(..., whereclause=..., values=...) in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Move legacy update constructor kwargs onto the statement object with .where(...).values(...).
guides.summary: The supported update case is syntax cleanup, not a semantic query rewrite.
guides.answer: Build the update statement first, then chain .where(expr) and .values({...}) on it.
guides.before_code:
stmt = update(
    user_table,
    whereclause=user_table.c.id == user_id,
    values={"name": name},
)
guides.after_code:
stmt = (
    update(user_table)
    .where(user_table.c.id == user_id)
    .values({"name": name})
)
symptoms: Legacy whereclause and values constructor kwargs still show up in Core code.
The replacement shape is stable in the supported subset.
Teams want the repetitive update cleanup removed from code review.
covers: Direct update(table, whereclause=..., values=...) rewrites.
Repo reporting for the legacy update syntax bucket.
Cross-links to the insert and delete constructor pages.
manual_review: Helper wrappers that compute where clauses indirectly.
Generated code where the update call is assembled dynamically.
Files that also contain unsupported transaction-bound changes.

guides.h1: Why .eslintrc.js is a manual-review case for flat config migration
guides.description: Understand why JS-backed ESLint configs are out of scope for deterministic flat-config migration.
guides.summary: JS config files look close to static config until they are not. Good tooling should report them, not execute them.
guides.answer: Treat .eslintrc.js and its cousins as a repo-fit boundary. The honest move is to detect them, report them, and leave them untouched.
guides.after_code:
// manual review required
// keep logic-bearing config out of the deterministic path
symptoms: The repo config contains logic, not static data.
The migration needs a clean red flag instead of a false promise.
Teams want to know fast whether the repo is a fit.
covers: Explicit unsupported-js-config findings.
Fast repo qualification before buying the flat-config pack.
Links to the supported static-config guides.
manual_review: Any JS config that computes env, plugins, or rules.
Module imports inside the config file.
Repos that already started a custom flat config migration.

guides.title: Move .eslintignore patterns into flat config
guides.h1: How to move .eslintignore patterns into flat config
guides.description: Carry straightforward .eslintignore and ignorePatterns entries into flat config while keeping the hard cases explicit.
guides.summary: Simple ignore patterns are portable. Negated and malformed patterns are not.
guides.answer: Move straightforward ignore patterns into the flat config ignores list. Stop when a pattern is negated or otherwise ambiguous.
symptoms: The repo still relies on .eslintignore or ignorePatterns.
The migration needs a clean carry-forward path for simple ignores.
Reviewers want to know which ignore rules stay safe and which do not.
covers: Straightforward .eslintignore pattern normalization.
Straightforward ignorePatterns migration from static configs.
Cross-links to the negated-pattern manual bucket.
manual_review: Negated ignore patterns.
Non-string ignorePatterns entries.
Ignore logic bundled with unsupported JS config files.

guides.title: Why negated ignore patterns need manual review in flat config migration
guides.h1: Why negated ignore patterns need manual review in flat config migration
guides.description: Understand why negated ignore rules belong in manual review instead of a guessed flat-config translation.
guides.summary: Simple ignore rules can move mechanically. Negated patterns need a closer read.
guides.answer: Treat negated ignore patterns as manual review. The target flat config behavior depends on how the rest of the ignore list is supposed to interact.
guides.after_code:
# manual review required
# negated ignore patterns are outside the deterministic flat-config subset
symptoms: The repo already uses exceptions inside .eslintignore or ignorePatterns.
A guessed migration can silently change which files are linted.
Teams want a clean stop signal instead of a risky translation.
covers: Explicit unsupported ignore-pattern findings.
Cross-links to the supported .eslintignore migration page.
Repo-fit guidance before buying the flat-config pack.
manual_review: Rebuilding the intended include/exclude behavior in flat config.

guides.title: Why multiple legacy ESLint config sources block flat config migration
guides.h1: Why multiple legacy ESLint config sources block flat config migration
guides.description: If a repo has more than one legacy ESLint config source, the deterministic migration path should stop for manual review.
guides.search_term: multiple ESLint config sources flat config migration
guides.summary: One static config source is a fit. Multiple overlapping sources are not a clean automatic migration target.
guides.answer: Keep multiple legacy ESLint config sources in manual review. The first job is deciding which source is authoritative before generating a flat config bridge.
guides.after_code:
# manual review required
# choose the authoritative legacy config source before migration
symptoms: The repo has both .eslintrc data and package.json eslintConfig.
Teams want one pass that qualifies repo fit before generating anything.
Reviewers need a clear reason why the flat-config pack stopped.
covers: Explicit multiple-config-sources findings.
Cross-links to the supported .eslintrc and package.json pages.
Repo-fit guidance before buying the flat-config pack.
manual_review: Choosing the real source of truth.
Merging overlapping rules and ignores.
Repos that also already have eslint.config.* files.

guides.title: What to do when eslint.config.* already exists
guides.h1: What to do when eslint.config.* already exists
guides.description: If the repo already has eslint.config.js, .cjs, or .mjs, the static legacy-config migration path should stop for manual review.
guides.summary: If the repo already has a flat config file, you are not starting from the simple bridge case anymore.
guides.answer: Treat existing eslint.config.* files as manual review. The next step depends on whether the flat config is authoritative, partial, or mid-migration.
guides.after_code:
# manual review required
# repo already has a flat config file
symptoms: The repo already contains eslint.config.js, .cjs, or .mjs.
The flat-config pack needs to stop before generating duplicate or conflicting output.
Teams want fast repo qualification instead of accidental config churn.
covers: Explicit existing-flat-config findings.
Cross-links to the supported static legacy-config pages.
Repo-fit guidance before buying the flat-config pack.
manual_review: Deciding whether the existing flat config is authoritative.
Cleaning up mixed legacy and flat-config states.
Repos that also have JS-backed legacy configs.

guides.title: Replace Query.as_scalar() with scalar_subquery() in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: How to replace Query.as_scalar() with scalar_subquery() in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Migrate legacy Query.subquery().as_scalar() calls to the scalar_subquery() method.
guides.summary: The as_scalar() method is gone. The replacement is scalar_subquery().
guides.answer: Replace .subquery().as_scalar() or .as_scalar() with .scalar_subquery() on the select or query.
guides.after_code:
subq = (
    select(func.count(Order.id))
    .where(Order.user_id == User.id)
    .scalar_subquery()
)
symptoms: Legacy queries still use .as_scalar() for correlated subqueries.
AttributeError on .as_scalar() after the 2.0 upgrade.
Teams need the scalar subquery cleanup bundled with other Query fixes.
covers: Direct .as_scalar() to .scalar_subquery() rewrites.
Reporting for where the legacy pattern still appears.
Cross-links to the select syntax cleanup page.
manual_review: Complex correlated subqueries tied to broader query redesign.
Cases where the subquery shape also needs correction.
Files mixed with unsupported execution-path changes.

guides.title: Wrap raw SQL in text() for SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: How to wrap raw SQL strings in text() for SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Raw SQL strings now need explicit text() wrapping in SQLAlchemy 2.0 execute calls.
guides.summary: SQLAlchemy 2.0 no longer auto-coerces string SQL. Use text() explicitly.
guides.answer: Wrap any raw SQL string passed to execute() in text() from sqlalchemy.
guides.before_code: result = conn.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = :id", {"id": 1})
guides.after_code:
from sqlalchemy import text

result = conn.execute(text("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = :id"), {"id": 1})
symptoms: ArgumentError about expected executable but got string.
Old helpers pass raw SQL to execute() calls.
Teams want the easy text() wrapping handled automatically.
covers: Detection of raw string SQL passed to execute().
text() import injection and wrapping.
Report entries for where raw SQL still appears.
manual_review: Dynamic SQL construction that mixes strings and expressions.
Helpers that reshape SQL arguments before execution.
Files bundled with engine.execute() removal.

guides.title: Adapt session.execute() result handling in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: How to adapt session.execute() result handling in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Session.execute() in 2.0 returns Result objects, not lists. Use .scalars() or .all() for ORM rows.
guides.summary: The result shape changed. Call .scalars().all() for ORM entity rows.
guides.answer: Call .scalars() on the Result to get ORM entity rows, then .all() or iterate.
guides.before_code: users = session.execute(select(User)).fetchall()  # returns list of Row
guides.after_code: users = session.execute(select(User)).scalars().all()  # returns list of User
symptoms: Code expects User objects but gets Row tuples.
Iteration over result gives tuples instead of mapped instances.
Teams need to update ORM result access patterns.
covers: Detection of legacy result-access patterns.
.scalars() insertion for ORM entity queries.
Cross-links to the select syntax cleanup page.
manual_review: Mixed column and entity queries that need custom unpacking.
Result-shape changes tied to broader query redesign.
Helpers that already wrap result processing.

guides.title: Replace backref with back_populates in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: How to replace backref strings with back_populates in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: The string backref pattern still works but explicit back_populates is the modern approach.
guides.summary: String backref is legacy. Explicit back_populates is cleaner for migration.
guides.answer: Keep backref if it works, or convert to explicit back_populates on both sides when the migration also touches the relationship.
symptoms: Reviewers prefer explicit back_populates over magic backref strings.
Migration is already touching the relationship definition.
Teams want both directions documented in code.
covers: Detection of string backref usage.
Reporting for where backref still appears.
Cross-links to the string loader cleanup pages.
manual_review: Complex relationship inheritance.
backref() with custom arguments.
Files where both sides are not easily accessible.

guides.title: Replace Extra.forbid with extra='forbid' in Pydantic v2
guides.h1: How to replace Extra.forbid with extra='forbid' in Pydantic v2
guides.description: Migrate the Extra enum usage to string literal in model_config.
guides.summary: The Extra enum is gone. Use string literals in model_config.
guides.answer: Replace Extra.forbid (and Extra.allow, Extra.ignore) with the string literal 'forbid', 'allow', or 'ignore' in model_config.
symptoms: ImportError for Extra from pydantic.
Models still reference Extra.forbid in nested Config.
Teams want the Extra cleanup bundled with Config migration.
covers: Extra.forbid/allow/ignore to string literal rewrites.
Nested Config to model_config conversion.

guides.description: Migrate schema_extra in Config to json_schema_extra in model_config.
guides.summary: schema_extra was renamed to json_schema_extra in Pydantic v2.
guides.answer: Replace schema_extra with json_schema_extra in your model_config.
symptoms: Schema generation ignores the old schema_extra key.

guides.title: Replace orm_mode with from_attributes in Pydantic v2
guides.h1: How to replace orm_mode=True with from_attributes=True in Pydantic v2
guides.description: The orm_mode config key was renamed to from_attributes in Pydantic v2.
guides.summary: orm_mode is now from_attributes. This is a simple rename.
guides.answer: Replace orm_mode=True with from_attributes=True in model_config.
guides.after_code:
class UserOut(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(from_attributes=True)
symptoms: Validation error when constructing model from ORM object.
Config still uses the legacy orm_mode key.
Teams need this rename bundled with other Config cleanup.

guides.title: Why Field(const=True) needs manual review in Pydantic v2
guides.h1: Why Field(const=True) needs manual review in Pydantic v2
guides.description: Field(const=True) was removed in Pydantic v2. Use Literal types instead.
guides.summary: const=True is gone. The replacement depends on how the constant should behave.
guides.answer: Replace Field(const=True, default=value) with a Literal type annotation. This changes the validation behavior.
guides.after_code:
from typing import Literal
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Config(BaseModel):
    version: Literal["1.0"] = "1.0"
symptoms: TypeError about unexpected keyword const.
Models need constant fields that reject other values.
Teams want clear guidance on the Literal replacement.
covers: Detection and reporting of Field(const=True) usage.
Guidance on the Literal type replacement.

guides.title: 'OptionEngine' object has no attribute 'execute' in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: Fix 'OptionEngine' object has no attribute 'execute' in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Fix the common OptionEngine execute error by moving execution onto a Connection or Session.
guides.summary: This error usually means code or a dependency still expects the removed Engine.execute path.
guides.answer: Open an explicit connection, wrap raw SQL in text(...), and pass the Connection into code that still expects an executable object.
guides.before_code:
rows = pd.read_sql_query("SELECT id FROM users", engine)
result = engine.execution_options(stream_results=True).execute(stmt)
guides.after_code:
from sqlalchemy import text

with engine.connect() as conn:
    rows = pd.read_sql_query(text("SELECT id FROM users"), conn)
    result = conn.execution_options(stream_results=True).execute(stmt)
symptoms: Pandas or helper code fails after upgrading SQLAlchemy.
The stack trace mentions OptionEngine.execute.
Execution options are chained from engine before execute(...).
covers: Detection of engine and OptionEngine execution paths.
Manual-review reporting for execution-boundary changes.
Links to the broader engine.execute removal guide.
manual_review: Transaction boundaries around writes.
Third-party libraries that need version-compatible adapters.
Raw SQL strings that should become text(...).

guides.title: 'Engine' object has no attribute 'execute' in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: Fix 'Engine' object has no attribute 'execute' in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Resolve Engine.execute attribute errors without guessing transaction behavior.
guides.summary: Engine.execute was removed, so the replacement depends on whether the code is reading, writing, or wrapping another library.
guides.answer: Use engine.connect() or engine.begin(), then execute on the returned Connection. Do not blindly replace engine.execute with session.execute.
guides.after_code:
with engine.connect() as conn:
    result = conn.execute(stmt)

with engine.begin() as conn:
    conn.execute(user_table.insert(), {"name": "Ada"})
symptoms: AttributeError appears immediately after the SQLAlchemy 2.0 bump.
Legacy database helpers call engine.execute directly.
Write paths relied on implicit autocommit behavior.
covers: Detection of removed engine.execute calls.
Separation of read and write examples.
Fail-closed reporting when transaction semantics are unclear.
manual_review: Choosing connect() versus begin().
Implicit autocommit replacements.
Helper APIs that hide the engine object.

guides.title: Fix LegacyAPIWarning for Query.get() in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: Fix LegacyAPIWarning: Query.get() is legacy in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Replace Query.get warnings with Session.get in the safe primary-key lookup subset.
guides.summary: Query.get is a low-risk cleanup when the call is a direct primary-key lookup.
guides.answer: Replace session.query(Model).get(primary_key) with session.get(Model, primary_key).
guides.after_code: user = session.get(User, user_id)
symptoms: Test output includes LegacyAPIWarning for Query.get().
Many files still use session.query(Model).get(...).
The migration has warning noise before real failures appear.
covers: Direct Query.get primary-key lookup rewrites.
Warning cleanup that keeps result shape stable.
Report entries for unsupported chained Query.get cases.
manual_review: Query chains before .get(...).
Non-primary-key lookups.
Custom query properties or session wrappers.

guides.title: Fix legacy select([..]) warnings in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.h1: Fix SQLAlchemy legacy select([..]) warnings
guides.description: Drop the old select list wrapper and use direct select(...) arguments.
guides.summary: select([..]) is syntax cleanup, not a business-logic migration.
guides.answer: Pass entities or columns directly into select(...), without the wrapper list.
guides.after_code: stmt = select(User.id, User.email)
symptoms: Warnings mention legacy calling style for select().
Old Core query builders still wrap columns in a list.
The same simple edit appears across many files.
covers: Direct select([..]) to select(..) rewrites.
Sitemap-linked explanation for warning cleanup.
Cross-links to DML constructor cleanup pages.
manual_review: Dynamically assembled select arguments.
Code generation templates.
Files that mix select syntax with execution-boundary changes.

guides.title: Fix joinedload_all is not defined during SQLAlchemy migration
guides.h1: Fix joinedload_all is not defined in SQLAlchemy 2.0
guides.description: Replace joinedload_all usage with chained joinedload calls when the path is obvious.
guides.summary: joinedload_all was removed, but only the simple relationship paths are safe to rewrite mechanically.
guides.answer: Use chained joinedload(...) calls with mapped attributes for clear paths. Stop for aliases, contains_eager, and duplicate join interactions.
symptoms: Importing joinedload_all fails after the upgrade.
Loader options still use dotted string paths.
The query also contains explicit joins or aliases.
covers: Simple joinedload_all detection and explanation.
Safe-path examples for mapped attributes.
Manual-review boundaries for query-shape-dependent loading.
manual_review: contains_eager usage.
Aliased classes in the same query.
Duplicate joins created by old helper functions.

guides.title: SQLAlchemy 2.0 migration triage checklist
guides.h1: SQLAlchemy 2.0 migration triage checklist
guides.description: Sort SQLAlchemy 2.0 migration work into safe codemod, manual review, and staged refactor buckets.
guides.summary: A migration moves faster when the easy syntax cleanup is separated from transaction and query-shape decisions.
guides.answer: Start with direct syntax cleanup, report execution-boundary changes, and leave broad Query-to-select rewrites for a deliberate stage.
guides.before_code:
1. Warnings and removed imports
2. Repetitive syntax cleanup
3. Execution-boundary failures
4. Query-shape refactors
guides.after_code:
codemod: Query.get, select([..]), simple string paths
manual review: engine.execute, from_self, multi-hop joins
later stage: broad Query-to-select redesign
symptoms: The repo has many different SQLAlchemy warnings at once.
Developers are mixing easy syntax edits with risky behavior changes.
Managers need a staging plan before approving migration time.
covers: A practical order for migration cleanup.
Which pages map to each decision bucket.
A scanner-first way to qualify product fit.
manual_review: Application-specific transaction rules.
Result-shape changes from ORM query rewrites.
Migrations that require domain knowledge.

guides.title: Manual SQLAlchemy migration vs codemod
guides.h1: Manual SQLAlchemy 2.0 migration vs codemod
guides.description: Decide when a SQLAlchemy 2.0 migration should use a deterministic codemod and when it should stay manual.
guides.summary: Codemods are valuable for repetitive syntax cleanup. They are risky when the right answer depends on runtime behavior.
guides.answer: Use a codemod for direct, syntax-aware changes. Keep transaction boundaries, alias-heavy loaders, and broad query redesign in manual review.
guides.before_code:
manual only:
    rewrite every query shape by hand
codemod only:
    guess through engine.execute and aliases
guides.after_code:
hybrid:
    automate direct syntax cleanup
    report ambiguous files
    validate before claiming success
symptoms: The team is unsure whether automation is safe.
A full manual migration looks too expensive.
A blanket AI refactor feels too risky for database code.
covers: A decision framework for codemod fit.
Examples of safe and blocked buckets.
Product positioning without broad migration promises.
manual_review: Business logic hidden in query construction.
Runtime-only behavior changes.
Validation failures after automated cleanup.

guides.title: Fix BaseSettings import errors in Pydantic v2

guides.title: Fix Pydantic v2 @validator deprecation warnings
guides.h1: Fix Pydantic v2 @validator deprecation warnings
guides.description: Replace simple @validator usage with @field_validator in the safe Pydantic v2 subset.

guides.title: Fix Pydantic v2 class Config deprecation warnings
guides.h1: Fix Pydantic v2 class Config deprecation warnings
guides.description: Convert safe class Config blocks into model_config with ConfigDict.
guides.summary: A nested class Config can become model_config when every key is in the supported rename subset.
guides.answer: Use ConfigDict and model_config for supported keys like extra, from_attributes, populate_by_name, and json_schema_extra.
